question: How do signal events differ from message events in their sender-to-recipient relationship?

options.text: Signals are a 1:N broadcast — all matching catch events fire — while messages are 1:1 and target a single recipient via a correlation key.
Both are 1:N broadcasts; the only difference is that signals match by name while messages match by correlation key.
Signals are 1:1 and require a correlation key; messages are 1:N broadcasts.
Both are 1:1; signals are scoped to the throwing process definition while messages may cross definitions.

a.text: The docs describe signals as a single-sender-to-several-recipient relationship and contrast this with messages, which are a single-sender-to-single-recipient relationship.

b.text: Messages are 1:1, not 1:N; the docs explicitly note that a message cannot have more than one recipient.

c.text: The roles are reversed: signals broadcast to many recipients, and messages are 1:1.

d.text: Signals are not scoped to the throwing process definition; broadcasting triggers all matching subscriptions, even across different processes.

explanation: Broadcasting a signal triggers every signal catch event whose name matches, even across different process definitions, so signals form a 1:N collaboration. Messages are used when a single process instance needs to wait for a message from a single sender, so they are 1:1 and correlate to one instance.
